title: The Mexican-American War
description: Test your skills on the causes and effects of the Mexican-American War!

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The great vice of the government is our violent party spirit; that vice which Washington foresaw, and against which his last warning was given; that which was the prominent evil of Rome, of Greece, of Holland, of all ancient and modern Republics, is our great calamity. This spirit of party, sordid, blind and selfish when carried to extremes, finds its choicest aliment in the local interests and sectional prejudices with which every country abounds. Those interests and prejudices necessarily increase with every extension of territory, and it is in this light that every great augmentation of the Union becomes formidable.”

>-Source: Theodore Sedgwick, *Thoughts on the Proposed Annexation of Texas to the United States,* 1844

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt emerged most directly from a larger intellectual debate over the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: extension of slavery into newly acquired territory.
lack of power of political parties to influence policy.
economic divide between the North and the South.
belief that the United States has a God-given right to expand.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The great vice of the government is our violent party spirit; that vice which Washington foresaw, and against which his last warning was given; that which was the prominent evil of Rome, of Greece, of Holland, of all ancient and modern Republics, is our great calamity. This spirit of party, sordid, blind and selfish when carried to extremes, finds its choicest aliment in the local interests and sectional prejudices with which every country abounds. Those interests and prejudices necessarily increase with every extension of territory, and it is in this light that every great augmentation of the Union becomes formidable.”

>-Source: Theodore Sedgwick, *Thoughts on the Proposed Annexation of Texas to the United States,* 1844

**The author’s ideas in the excerpt emerged most directly in response to which of the following developments in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the increased efforts to expand westward and acquire new land
the conflict over the renewal of the charter for the national bank
the end of the Whig Party and the rise of the Republican Party
the use of government funds to support Henry Clay’s American System

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The great vice of the government is our violent party spirit; that vice which Washington foresaw, and against which his last warning was given; that which was the prominent evil of Rome, of Greece, of Holland, of all ancient and modern Republics, is our great calamity. This spirit of party, sordid, blind and selfish when carried to extremes, finds its choicest aliment in the local interests and sectional prejudices with which every country abounds. Those interests and prejudices necessarily increase with every extension of territory, and it is in this light that every great augmentation of the Union becomes formidable.”

>-Source: Theodore Sedgwick, *Thoughts on the Proposed Annexation of Texas to the United States,* 1844

**The reference to “sectional prejudices” in the excerpt most directly refers to which of the following developments in politics during the mid-nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the regional differences on the issue of slavery
the United States’ efforts to expand westward
the nativist response to the rise of immigration
the rise of antebellum reform movements

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the long run, the most significant division of American opinion exacerbated by the war in Mexico was that between the North and South. The doctrine of America’s manifest destiny had not sprung originally from a slave power conspiracy but from policies with nationwide appeal and deep cultural roots. When James Knox Polk came into office, territorial expansion did not constitute a sectional issue but a party one. . . . Polk did not share Calhoun’s disposition to view all matters in terms of their impact on the slavery question. Nevertheless, as his term went by, his administration increasingly appeared narrowly southern in outlook. The president’s imperialist objectives came to prompt a bitter sectional dispute over slavery’s extension, bearing out Calhoun’s foreboding.”

>-Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that the Mexican-American War had on the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It increased sectionalism over the issue of slavery.
It led to the extension of slavery past Missouri.
It allowed the United States to expand to the Pacific Ocean.
It decreased public support for the abolitionist movement.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the long run, the most significant division of American opinion exacerbated by the war in Mexico was that between the North and South. The doctrine of America’s manifest destiny had not sprung originally from a slave power conspiracy but from policies with nationwide appeal and deep cultural roots. When James Knox Polk came into office, territorial expansion did not constitute a sectional issue but a party one. . . . Polk did not share Calhoun’s disposition to view all matters in terms of their impact on the slavery question. Nevertheless, as his term went by, his administration increasingly appeared narrowly southern in outlook. The president’s imperialist objectives came to prompt a bitter sectional dispute over slavery’s extension, bearing out Calhoun’s foreboding.”

>-Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**The pattern described in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following long-term developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the regional divisiveness within political parties
the renewed commitment to Manifest Destiny
the United States’ efforts to develop the West
the economic differences between regions

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the long run, the most significant division of American opinion exacerbated by the war in Mexico was that between the North and South. The doctrine of America’s manifest destiny had not sprung originally from a slave power conspiracy but from policies with nationwide appeal and deep cultural roots. When James Knox Polk came into office, territorial expansion did not constitute a sectional issue but a party one. . . . Polk did not share Calhoun’s disposition to view all matters in terms of their impact on the slavery question. Nevertheless, as his term went by, his administration increasingly appeared narrowly southern in outlook. The president’s imperialist objectives came to prompt a bitter sectional dispute over slavery’s extension, bearing out Calhoun’s foreboding.”

>-Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**Which of the following political shifts resulted most directly from the trends described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the realignment of political parties based on regional lines
the rise of third parties like the Know-Nothing Party
the supremacy of the Democratic Party in American politics
the shift toward slave states outnumbering free states

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Strike out the term *white,* and what will be the result? Hordes of Mexican Indians may come in here from the West and may be more formidable than the enemy you have vanquished. Silently they will come moving in; they will come back in thousands to Bexar, in thousands to Goliad, perhaps to Nacogdoches, and what will be the consequence? Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty thousand may come in here and vanquish you at the ballot box though you are invincible in arms . . . 

> “Talk not to me of democracy which brings the mean, grovelling  yellow race of Mexico . . . upon an equality of rights and privileges with the freeborn races of Europe. The God of nature has made them inferior; he has made the African and the red man inferior to the white.”

> -Source: Remarks from the Texas Constitutional Convention, as printed in *Distant Horizon: Documents from the Nineteenth-Century American West* (2000), 1845

**The ideas in the excerpt were most directly motivated by the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: concerns about the legal status of non-white people in acquired territory
support for the ratification of an amendment to define citizenship
desire to institute a literacy test for all state and federal elections
tensions created by the outcome of the Mexican-American War

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Strike out the term *white,* and what will be the result? Hordes of Mexican Indians may come in here from the West and may be more formidable than the enemy you have vanquished. Silently they will come moving in; they will come back in thousands to Bexar, in thousands to Goliad, perhaps to Nacogdoches, and what will be the consequence? Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty thousand may come in here and vanquish you at the ballot box though you are invincible in arms . . . 

> “Talk not to me of democracy which brings the mean, grovelling  yellow race of Mexico . . . upon an equality of rights and privileges with the freeborn races of Europe. The God of nature has made them inferior; he has made the African and the red man inferior to the white.”

> -Source: Remarks from the Texas Constitutional Convention, as printed in *Distant Horizon: Documents from the Nineteenth-Century American West* (2000), 1845

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical events?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the acquisition of new territories in the West
the end of the Mexican-American War
the passage of the Missouri Compromise
the revolution of Texas against Mexico

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Strike out the term *white,* and what will be the result? Hordes of Mexican Indians may come in here from the West and may be more formidable than the enemy you have vanquished. Silently they will come moving in; they will come back in thousands to Bexar, in thousands to Goliad, perhaps to Nacogdoches, and what will be the consequence? Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty thousand may come in here and vanquish you at the ballot box though you are invincible in arms . . . 

> “Talk not to me of democracy which brings the mean, grovelling  yellow race of Mexico . . . upon an equality of rights and privileges with the freeborn races of Europe. The God of nature has made them inferior; he has made the African and the red man inferior to the white.”

> -Source: Remarks from the Texas Constitutional Convention, as printed in *Distant Horizon: Documents from the Nineteenth-Century American West* (2000), 1845

**The excerpt would be most useful to historians as a source of information about which of the following?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the relationship between white Americans and the Mexican and indigenous population in the newly annexed territories
the allocation of power between the federal government and state governments on questions of the extension of slavery
the rise of nativist attitudes towards immigrants who moved to newly-acquired territory in the West
the role that new federal legislation played in encouraging families to move to the Great Plains

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b3e7966f3575d38e3650e68088b4dc67.png)

>-Source: Edward Hooper, “The Hounds of San Francisco attacking Little Chile,” published in [The *Annals of San Francisco,*](https://play.google.com/store/books/details?id=VMYUAAAAYAAJ&rdid=book-VMYUAAAAYAAJ&rdot=1) 1855

**The situation depicted in the image best serves as evidence of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in conflict between white Americans and the residents of the Mexican Cession territory
the surge in nativism following increased immigration to new territories in the West
the increase in disputes over the extension of slavery into territory gained after the Mexican-American War
the competition over land that ensued after the passage of the Homestead Act

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b3e7966f3575d38e3650e68088b4dc67.png)

>-Source: Edward Hooper, “The Hounds of San Francisco attacking Little Chile,” published in [The *Annals of San Francisco,*](https://play.google.com/store/books/details?id=VMYUAAAAYAAJ&rdid=book-VMYUAAAAYAAJ&rdot=1) 1855

**The image was created most directly in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rising tension between racial groups following mass American migration to the West
the passage of legislation that allowed the extension of slavery into land acquired from Mexico
the start of a financial panic that caused the price of agricultural goods to drop dramatically
the United States’ entrance into the Mexican-American War after a series of border disputes

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It must be apparent to our Castilian [Spanish-speaking] friends, that it is not only their interest but absolute duty to give their children an English education; and to learn, if possible, to read and speak the language themselves. We are now under the American flag, whether of our own accord or per force, and there is every probability that we shall remain so for all time to come . . . we are Native California Americans born on the soil and we can exclaim with the Poet, this is ‘OUR OWN, OUR NATIVE LAND.’  But the sooner we become ‘Americanized,’ . . . the better it will be for us and our posterity. . . . let us divest ourselves of all bygone traditions, and become Americanized all over— in language, in manners, in customs and habits.” 

> -Source: Francisco Ramírez, editorial in *El Clamor Publico*, 1859


**The author’s account in the excerpt above most directly encouraged which of the following changes in subsequent years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the adoption of some American cultural traits to avoid discrimination
the rise of Mexican-American resistance groups who organized against unjust laws
the creation of residential schools to separate Mexican-American children from their parents’ culture
the extension of citizenship to Mexicans living in territory acquired after the Mexican-American War
